[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/FrankF%C3%BCrstGrab.JPG/250px-FrankF%C3%BCrstGrab.JPG)](/wiki/File:FrankF%C3%BCrstGrab.JPG)

[Frankish leader grave](/wiki/Gellep-Stratum "Gellep-Stratum"), c. 500, with golden [Spangenhelm](/wiki/Spangenhelm "Spangenhelm") in [Krefeld](/wiki/Krefeld "Krefeld"), Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Frankish_-_Digitated_Fibula_-_Walters_542443.jpg/250px-Frankish_-_Digitated_Fibula_-_Walters_542443.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankish_-_Digitated_Fibula_-_Walters_542443.jpg)

A 6th century bow [fibula](/wiki/Fibula_(brooch) "Fibula (brooch)") found in north-eastern France and the Rhineland. They were worn by Frankish noblewomen in pairs at the shoulder or as belt ornaments.

Childeric and his son [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") faced competition from the Roman [Aegidius](/wiki/Aegidius "Aegidius") as competitor for the "kingship" of the Franks associated with the Roman Loire forces (according to [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours"), Aegidius held the kingship of the Franks for 8 years while Childeric was in exile). This new type of kingship, perhaps inspired by [Alaric I](/wiki/Alaric_I "Alaric I"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) represents the start of the [Merovingian dynasty](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") which succeeded in conquering most of Gaul in the 6th century, as well as establishing its leadership over all the Frankish kingdoms on the Rhine frontier. Aegidius died in 464 or 465.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Childeric and his son Clovis I were both described as rulers of the Roman Province of *[Belgica Secunda](/wiki/Belgica_Secunda "Belgica Secunda")*, by its spiritual leader in the time of Clovis, [Saint Remigius](/wiki/Saint_Remigius "Saint Remigius").

Clovis later defeated the son of Aegidius, [Syagrius](/wiki/Syagrius "Syagrius"), in 486 or 487 and then had the Frankish king [Chararic](/wiki/Chararic_(Frankish_king) "Chararic (Frankish king)") imprisoned and executed. A few years later, he killed [Ragnachar](/wiki/Ragnachar "Ragnachar"), the Frankish king of Cambrai, and his brothers. After conquering the [Kingdom of Soissons](/wiki/Domain_of_Soissons "Domain of Soissons") and expelling the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") from southern Gaul at the [Battle of Vouillé](/wiki/Battle_of_Vouill%C3%A9 "Battle of Vouillé"), he established Frankish hegemony over most of Gaul, excluding [Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy "Kingdom of Burgundy"), Provence and [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), which were eventually absorbed by his successors. By the 490s, he had conquered all the Frankish kingdoms to the west of the [River Maas](/wiki/River_Maas "River Maas") except for the [Ripuarian Franks](/wiki/Ripuarian_Franks "Ripuarian Franks") and was in a position to make the city of Paris his capital. He became the first king of all Franks in 509, after he had conquered Cologne.

Clovis I divided his realm between his four sons, who united to defeat Burgundy in 534. Internecine feuding occurred during the reigns of the brothers [Sigebert I](/wiki/Sigebert_I "Sigebert I") and [Chilperic I](/wiki/Chilperic_I "Chilperic I"), which was largely fuelled by the rivalry of their queens, [Brunhilda](/wiki/Brunhilda_of_Austrasia "Brunhilda of Austrasia") and [Fredegunda](/wiki/Fredegunda "Fredegunda"), and which continued during the reigns of their sons and their grandsons. Three distinct subkingdoms emerged: [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia"), [Neustria](/wiki/Neustria "Neustria") and Burgundy, each of which developed independently and sought to exert influence over the others. The influence of the [Arnulfing](/wiki/Arnulfing "Arnulfing") clan of Austrasia ensured that the political centre of gravity in the kingdom gradually shifted eastwards to the Rhineland.

The Frankish realm was reunited in 613 by [Chlothar II](/wiki/Chlothar_II "Chlothar II"), the son of Chilperic, who granted his nobles the [Edict of Paris](/wiki/Edict_of_Paris "Edict of Paris") in an effort to reduce corruption and reassert his authority. Following the military successes of his son and successor [Dagobert I](/wiki/Dagobert_I "Dagobert I"), royal authority rapidly declined under a series of kings, traditionally known as *les [rois fainéants](/wiki/Rois_fain%C3%A9ants "Rois fainéants")*. After the [Battle of Tertry](/wiki/Battle_of_Tertry "Battle of Tertry") in 687, each [mayor of the palace](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_palace "Mayor of the palace"), who had formerly been the king's chief household official, effectively held power until in 751, with the approval of the Pope and the nobility, [Pepin the Short](/wiki/Pepin_the_Short "Pepin the Short") deposed the last Merovingian king [Childeric III](/wiki/Childeric_III "Childeric III") and had himself crowned. This inaugurated a new dynasty, the [Carolingians](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty").

### Carolingian kingdom (751–987)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Carolingian kingdom (751–987)")]

Main articles: [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty") and [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Carolingian_Warrior.jpg/250px-Carolingian_Warrior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carolingian_Warrior.jpg)

[Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") warrior on a [war horse](/wiki/Horses_in_warfare "Horses in warfare") (8th to 10th century) with [lance](/wiki/Lance "Lance"), [round shield](/wiki/Shields "Shields"), [chainmail](/wiki/Mail_(armour) "Mail (armour)") and [spangenhelm](/wiki/Spangenhelm "Spangenhelm") in the Coronation Hall of the [Aachen City Hall](/wiki/Aachen_Rathaus "Aachen Rathaus") in June 2014 on the occasion of the exhibition "Charlemagne – Power, Art, Treasures"

The unification achieved by the Merovingians ensured the continuation of what has become known as the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance"). The Carolingian Empire was beset by internecine warfare, but the combination of Frankish rule and Roman Christianity ensured that it was fundamentally united. Frankish government and culture depended very much upon each ruler and his aims and so each region of the empire developed differently. Although a ruler's aims depended upon the political alliances of his family, the leading families of Francia shared the same basic beliefs and ideas of government, which had both Roman and Germanic roots.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Frankish state consolidated its hold over the majority of western Europe by the end of the 8th century, developing into the Carolingian Empire. With the [coronation of their ruler Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne#Coronation "Charlemagne") as [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") by [Pope Leo III](/wiki/Pope_Leo_III "Pope Leo III") in 800, he and his successors were recognised as legitimate successors to the emperors of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"). As such, the Carolingian Empire gradually came to be seen in the West as a continuation of the ancient Roman Empire. This empire would give rise to several successor states, including France, the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and [Burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy "Burgundy").

After the death of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), his only adult surviving son became Emperor and King [Louis the Pious](/wiki/Louis_the_Pious "Louis the Pious"). Following Louis the Pious's death, however, according to Frankish culture and law that demanded equality among all living male adult heirs, the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") was now split between Louis' three sons.

## Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Military")]

### Participation in the Roman army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Participation in the Roman army")]

Germanic peoples, including those tribes in the Rhine delta that later became the Franks, are known to have served in the Roman army since the days of [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar"). After the Roman administration collapsed in Gaul in the 260s, the armies under the Germanic Batavian [Postumus](/wiki/Postumus "Postumus") revolted and proclaimed him emperor and then restored order. From then on, Germanic soldiers in the Roman army, most notably Franks, were promoted from the ranks. A few decades later, the Menapian [Carausius](/wiki/Carausius "Carausius") proclaimed himself a co-emperor and based himself in Britain. His military included Frankish soldiers. Later Frankish soldiers such as [Magnentius](/wiki/Magnentius "Magnentius"), [Silvanus](/wiki/Claudius_Silvanus "Claudius Silvanus"), [Ricomer](/wiki/Ricomer "Ricomer") and [Bauto](/wiki/Bauto "Bauto") held command positions in the Roman army during the mid 4th century. From the narrative of [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus") it is evident that both Frankish and Alamannic tribal armies were organised along Roman lines.

In the fifth century, the Roman armies at the Rhine border became a Frankish "franchise", and Franks were known to levy Roman-like troops that were supported by a Roman-like armour and weapons industry. This lasted at least until the days of the scholar [Procopius](/wiki/Procopius "Procopius") (c. 500 – c. 565), more than a century after the demise of the Western Roman Empire, who wrote describing the former *[Arborychoi](/wiki/Arborychoi "Arborychoi")*, having merged with the Franks, retaining their legionary organization in the style of their forefathers during Roman times.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Franks under the Merovingians melded Germanic custom with Romanised organisation and several important tactical innovations.

### Military practices of the early Franks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Military practices of the early Franks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Halle_%28Saale%29%2C_Landesmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Vorgeschichte%2C_das_Monument_von_Hornhausen.jpg/250px-Halle_%28Saale%29%2C_Landesmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Vorgeschichte%2C_das_Monument_von_Hornhausen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halle_(Saale),_Landesmuseum_f%C3%BCr_Vorgeschichte,_das_Monument_von_Hornhausen.jpg)

The [Hornhausen rider stone](/wiki/Hornhausen_rider_stone "Hornhausen rider stone") is a 7th-century relief depicting a Frankish warrior on horseback with shield, longsword, and lance.

The primary sources for Frankish military custom and armament are [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus"), [Agathias](/wiki/Agathias "Agathias") and Procopius, the latter two [Eastern Roman](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") historians writing about Frankish intervention in the [Gothic War](/wiki/Gothic_War_(535%E2%80%93552) "Gothic War (535–552)").

Writing of 539, Procopius says:

> At this time the Franks, hearing that both the Goths and Romans had suffered severely by the war ... forgetting for the moment their oaths and treaties ... (for this nation in matters of trust is the most treacherous in the world), they straightway gathered to the number of one hundred thousand under the leadership of [Theudebert I](/wiki/Theudebert_I "Theudebert I") and marched into Italy: they had a small body of cavalry about their leader, and these were the only ones armed with spears, while all the rest were foot soldiers having neither bows nor spears, but each man carried a sword and shield and one axe. Now the iron head of this weapon was thick and exceedingly sharp on both sides, while the wooden handle was very short. And they are accustomed always to throw these axes at a signal in the first charge and thus to shatter the shields of the enemy and kill the men.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

His contemporary, Agathias, who based his own writings upon the tropes laid down by Procopius, says:

> The military equipment of this people [the Franks] is very simple ... They do not know the use of the [coat of mail](/wiki/Coat_of_mail "Coat of mail") or [greaves](/wiki/Greave "Greave") and the majority leave the head uncovered, only a few wear the helmet. They have their chests bare and backs naked to the loins, they cover their thighs with either leather or linen. They do not serve on horseback except in very rare cases. Fighting on foot is both habitual and a national custom and they are proficient in this. At the hip they wear a sword and on the left side their shield is attached. They have neither bows nor slings, no missile weapons except the double edged axe and the [angon](/wiki/Angon "Angon") which they use most often. The angons are spears which are neither very short nor very long. They can be used, if necessary, for throwing like a [javelin](/wiki/Javelin "Javelin"), and also in [hand to hand combat](/wiki/Hand_to_hand_combat "Hand to hand combat").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In the *[Strategikon](/wiki/Strategikon_of_Maurice "Strategikon of Maurice")*, supposedly written by the [emperor Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_(emperor) "Maurice (emperor)"), or in his time, the Franks are lumped together with the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") under the heading of the "fair-haired" peoples.

> If they are hard pressed in cavalry actions, they dismount at a single prearranged sign and line up on foot. Although only a few against many horsemen, they do not shrink from the fight. They are armed with shields, lances, and short swords slung from their shoulders. They prefer fighting on foot and rapid charges. [...] Either on horseback or on foot they are impetuous and undisciplined in charging, as if they were the only people in the world who are not cowards.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

While the above quotations have been used as a statement of the military practices of the Frankish nation in the 6th century and have even been extrapolated to the entire period preceding [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel")'s reforms (early mid-8th century), post-Second World War historiography has emphasised the inherited Roman characteristics of the Frankish military from the date of the beginning of the conquest of Gaul. The Byzantine authors present several contradictions and difficulties. Procopius denies the Franks the use of the spear while Agathias makes it one of their primary weapons. They agree that the Franks were primarily infantrymen, threw axes and carried a sword and shield. Both writers also contradict the authority of Gallic authors of the same general time period ([Sidonius Apollinaris](/wiki/Sidonius_Apollinaris "Sidonius Apollinaris") and [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours")) and the archaeological evidence. The *[Lex Ribuaria](/wiki/Lex_Ribuaria "Lex Ribuaria")*, the early 7th century legal code of the Rhineland or Ripuarian Franks, specifies the values of various goods when paying a [wergild](/wiki/Wergild "Wergild") in kind; whereas a spear and shield were worth only two *[solidi](/wiki/Solidi "Solidi")*, a sword and scabbard were valued at seven, a helmet at six, and a "metal tunic" at twelve.[[68]](#cite_note-James-68) [Scramasaxes](/wiki/Scramasax "Scramasax") and arrowheads are numerous in Frankish graves even though the Byzantine historians do not assign them to the Franks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Gr%C3%A9goire_de_Tours%2C_Histoire_des_Francs%2C_livres_1_%C3%A0_6%2C_page_de_frontispice.jpg/250px-Gr%C3%A9goire_de_Tours%2C_Histoire_des_Francs%2C_livres_1_%C3%A0_6%2C_page_de_frontispice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gr%C3%A9goire_de_Tours,_Histoire_des_Francs,_livres_1_%C3%A0_6,_page_de_frontispice.jpg)

The frontispiece of Gregory's *Historia Francorum*

The evidence of Gregory and of the *[Lex Salica](/wiki/Lex_Salica "Lex Salica")* implies that the early Franks were a cavalry people. In fact, some modern historians have hypothesised that the Franks possessed so numerous a body of horses that they could use them to plough fields and thus were agriculturally technologically advanced over their neighbours. The *[Lex Ribuaria](/wiki/Lex_Ribuaria "Lex Ribuaria")* specifies that a mare's value was the same as that of an ox or of a shield and spear, two *solidi* and a stallion seven or the same as a sword and scabbard,[[68]](#cite_note-James-68) which suggests that horses were relatively common. Perhaps the Byzantine writers considered the Frankish horse to be insignificant relative to the Greek cavalry, which is probably accurate.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Merovingian military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Merovingian military")]

#### Composition and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Composition and development")]

The Frankish military establishment incorporated many of the pre-existing Roman institutions in Gaul, especially during and after the conquests of Clovis I in the late 5th and early 6th centuries. Frankish military strategy revolved around the holding and taking of fortified centres (*castra*) and in general these centres were held by garrisons of *milities* and *[laeti](/wiki/Laeti "Laeti")*, who were descendants of Roman soldiers with Germanic origin, granted a quasi-national status under Frankish law. These *milites* continued to be commanded by tribunes.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Throughout Gaul, the descendants of Roman soldiers continued to wear their uniforms and perform their ceremonial duties.

Immediately beneath the Frankish king in the military hierarchy were the *leudes*, his sworn followers, who were generally 'old soldiers' in service away from court.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The king had an elite bodyguard called the *[truste](/wiki/Truste "Truste")*. Members of the *truste* often served in *centannae*, garrison settlements that were established for military and police purposes. The day-to-day bodyguard of the king was made up of *[antrustiones](/wiki/Antrustiones "Antrustiones")* (senior soldiers who were [aristocrats](/wiki/Aristocracy_(class) "Aristocracy (class)") in military service) and *pueri* (junior soldiers and not aristocrats).[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall200748–49-72) All high-ranking men had *pueri*.

The Frankish military was not composed solely of Franks and Gallo-Romans, but also contained [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans"), [Taifals](/wiki/Taifals "Taifals") and [Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni"). After the conquest of [Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy "Kingdom of Burgundy") (534), the well-organised military institutions of that kingdom were integrated into the Frankish realm. Chief among these was the standing army under the command of the [Patrician of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy").

In the late 6th century, during the wars instigated by [Fredegund](/wiki/Fredegund "Fredegund") and [Brunhilda](/wiki/Brunhilda_of_Austrasia "Brunhilda of Austrasia"), the Merovingian monarchs introduced a new element into their militaries: the local [levy](/wiki/Feudal_levies "Feudal levies"). A levy consisted of all the able-bodied men of a district who were required to report for military service when called upon, similar to [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription"). The local levy applied only to a city and its environs. Initially only in certain cities in western Gaul, in Neustria and Aquitaine, did the kings possess the right or power to call up the levy. The commanders of the local levies were always different from the commanders of the urban garrisons. Often the former were commanded by the [counts](/wiki/Comes "Comes") of the districts. A much rarer occurrence was the general levy, which applied to the entire kingdom and included peasants (*pauperes* and *inferiores*). General levies could also be made within the still-pagan trans-Rhenish [stem duchies](/wiki/Stem_duchies "Stem duchies") on the orders of a monarch. The [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), Alemanni and [Thuringi](/wiki/Thuringi "Thuringi") all had the institution of the levy and the Frankish monarchs could depend upon their levies until the mid-7th century, when the stem dukes began to sever their ties to the monarchy. [Radulf of Thuringia](/wiki/Radulf,_King_of_Thuringia "Radulf, King of Thuringia") called up the levy for a war against [Sigebert III](/wiki/Sigebert_III "Sigebert III") in 640.

Soon the local levy spread to Austrasia and the less Romanised regions of Gaul. On an intermediate level, the kings began calling up territorial levies from the regions of Austrasia (which did not have major cities of Roman origin). All the forms of the levy gradually disappeared, however, in the course of the 7th century after the reign of [Dagobert I](/wiki/Dagobert_I "Dagobert I"). Under the so-called *[rois fainéants](/wiki/Rois_fain%C3%A9ants "Rois fainéants")*, the levies disappeared by mid-century in Austrasia and later in Burgundy and Neustria. Only in Aquitaine, which was fast becoming independent of the central Frankish monarchy, did complex military institutions persist into the 8th century. In the final half of the 7th century and first half of the 8th in Merovingian Gaul, the chief military actors became the lay and ecclesiastical [magnates](/wiki/Magnate "Magnate") with their bands of armed followers called retainers. The other aspects of the Merovingian military, mostly Roman in origin or innovations of powerful kings, disappeared from the scene by the 8th century.

#### Strategy, tactics and equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Strategy, tactics and equipment")]

Merovingian armies used [coats of mail](/wiki/Hauberk "Hauberk"), helmets, [shields](/wiki/Shield "Shield"), [lances](/wiki/Lance "Lance"), [swords](/wiki/Sword "Sword"), [bows and arrows](/wiki/Bow_and_arrow "Bow and arrow") and [war horses](/wiki/Horses_in_warfare "Horses in warfare"). The armament of private armies resembled those of the Gallo-Roman *potentiatores* of the late Empire. A strong element of Alanic cavalry settled in [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica") influenced the fighting style of the [Bretons](/wiki/Bretons "Bretons") down into the 12th century. Local urban levies could be reasonably well-armed and even mounted, but the more general levies were composed of *pauperes* and *inferiores*, who were mostly farmers by trade and carried ineffective weapons, such as farming implements. The peoples east of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") – Franks, Saxons and even [Wends](/wiki/Wends "Wends") – who were sometimes called upon to serve, wore rudimentary armour and carried weapons such as [spears](/wiki/Spear "Spear") and [axes](/wiki/Axe "Axe"). Few of these men were mounted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Merovingian society had a militarised nature. The Franks called annual meetings every [Marchfeld](/wiki/Marchfield_(Frankish) "Marchfield (Frankish)") (1 March), when the king and his nobles assembled in large open fields and determined their targets for the next campaigning season. The meetings were a show of strength on behalf of the monarch and a way for him to retain loyalty among his troops.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall200743-73) In their civil wars, the Merovingian kings concentrated on the holding of fortified places and the use of [siege engines](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine"). In wars waged against external foes, the objective was typically the acquisition of booty or the enforcement of tribute. Only in the lands beyond the Rhine did the Merovingians seek to extend political control over their neighbours.

Tactically, the Merovingians borrowed heavily from the Romans, especially regarding siege warfare. Their battle tactics were highly flexible and were designed to meet the specific circumstances of a battle. The tactic of subterfuge was employed endlessly. Cavalry formed a large segment of an army,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but troops readily dismounted to fight on foot. The Merovingians were capable of raising naval forces: the naval campaign waged against the [Danes](/wiki/Danes_(Germanic_tribe) "Danes (Germanic tribe)") by [Theuderic I](/wiki/Theuderic_I "Theuderic I") in 515 involved ocean-worthy ships and rivercraft were used on the [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire"), [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") and [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Frankish language](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png/330px-Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)

[Germanic tribes](/wiki/List_of_early_Germanic_peoples "List of early Germanic peoples") in the 5th century

In a modern [linguistic](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") context, the [Germanic language](/wiki/Germanic_language "Germanic language") of the early Franks is variously called "Old Frankish" or "Old Franconian" and these terms refer to the language of the Franks prior to the advent of the [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift"), which took place in the 7th century. After this consonant shift the Frankish dialects diverged. The dialects which would become modern [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") did not undergo the consonantal shift, while all others did so, to varying degrees, creating the so-called [Rhenish fan](/wiki/Rhenish_fan "Rhenish fan") pattern.[[74]](#cite_note-74) As a result, the distinction between [Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch") and Old Frankish is largely negligible, and "Old Dutch" (also called "[Old Low Franconian](/wiki/Old_Low_Franconian "Old Low Franconian")") is the term used to distinguish the variants which were not affected by this Second Germanic consonant shift.[[75]](#cite_note-mees-75)

The early Frankish language has not been directly attested, apart from a very small number of [runic inscriptions](/wiki/Elder_Futhark "Elder Futhark") found within contemporary Frankish territory such as the [Bergakker inscription](/wiki/Bergakker_inscription "Bergakker inscription"). Nevertheless, a significant amount of Frankish vocabulary has been reconstructed by examining early Germanic loanwords found in [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") as well as through [comparative reconstruction](/wiki/Comparative_method_(linguistics) "Comparative method (linguistics)") through Dutch.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-britannica.com-77) The influence of Old Frankish on contemporary [Gallo-Roman](/wiki/Gallo-Roman "Gallo-Roman") [vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary") and [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"), have long been the subjects of scholarly debate.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Frankish influence is thought to include the designations of the four cardinal directions: *nord* "north", *sud* "south", *est* "east" and *ouest* "west" and at least an additional 1000 stem words.[[77]](#cite_note-britannica.com-77)

Although the Franks would eventually conquer all of [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), speakers of Frankish apparently expanded in sufficient numbers only into northern Gaul to have a linguistic effect. For several centuries, northern Gaul was a bilingual territory ([Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") and Frankish). The language used in writing, in government and by the Church was Latin. [Urban T. Holmes](/wiki/Urban_T._Holmes,_Jr. "Urban T. Holmes, Jr.") has proposed that a Germanic language continued to be spoken as a second tongue by public officials in western [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia") and Northern [Neustria](/wiki/Neustria "Neustria") as late as the 850s, and that it completely disappeared as a spoken language during the 10th century from regions where only French is spoken today.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The Germanic tribes who were called Franks in [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") are associated with the [Weser–Rhine Germanic](/wiki/Weser%E2%80%93Rhine_Germanic "Weser–Rhine Germanic")/[Istvaeonic](/wiki/Istvaeonic "Istvaeonic") cultural-linguistic grouping.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-H._Schutz_2001,_p.42-82)

### Art and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Art and architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_04.JPG/250px-Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_04.JPG)

A [chalice](/wiki/Chalice "Chalice") from the [Treasure of Gourdon](/wiki/Treasure_of_Gourdon "Treasure of Gourdon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Aachener_Dom_BW_2016-07-09_16-20-40.jpg/250px-Aachener_Dom_BW_2016-07-09_16-20-40.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aachener_Dom_BW_2016-07-09_16-20-40.jpg)

The pinnacle of Carolingian architecture: The [Palatine chapel](/wiki/Palatine_Chapel_in_Aachen "Palatine Chapel in Aachen") in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), Germany

Main articles: [Merovingian art and architecture](/wiki/Merovingian_art_and_architecture "Merovingian art and architecture") and [Carolingian art](/wiki/Carolingian_art "Carolingian art")

Early Frankish art and architecture belongs to a phase known as [Migration Period art](/wiki/Migration_Period_art "Migration Period art"), which has left very few remains. The later period is called [Carolingian art](/wiki/Carolingian_art "Carolingian art"), or, especially in architecture, [pre-Romanesque](/wiki/Pre-Romanesque "Pre-Romanesque"). Very little Merovingian architecture has been preserved. The earliest churches seem to have been timber-built, with larger examples being of a [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") type. The most completely surviving example, a [baptistery](/wiki/Baptistery "Baptistery") in [Poitiers](/wiki/Poitiers "Poitiers"), is a building with three [apses](/wiki/Apse "Apse") of a Gallo-Roman style. A number of small baptistries can be seen in [Southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"): as these fell out of fashion, they were not updated and have subsequently survived as they were.

Jewelry (such as brooches), weapons (including swords with decorative hilts) and clothing (such as capes and sandals) have been found in a number of grave sites. The grave of Queen [Aregund](/wiki/Aregund "Aregund"), discovered in 1959, and the [Treasure of Gourdon](/wiki/Treasure_of_Gourdon "Treasure of Gourdon"), which was deposited soon after 524, are notable examples. The few [Merovingian illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Merovingian_illuminated_manuscript "Merovingian illuminated manuscript") that have survived, such as the [Gelasian Sacramentary](/wiki/Gelasian_Sacramentary "Gelasian Sacramentary"), contain a great deal of [zoomorphic representations](/wiki/Zoomorphism "Zoomorphism"). Such Frankish objects show a greater use of the style and motifs of [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") and a lesser degree of skill and sophistication in design and manufacture than comparable works from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). So little has survived, however, that the best quality of work from this period may not be represented.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The objects produced by the main centres of the Carolingian Renaissance, which represent a transformation from that of the earlier period, have survived in far greater quantity. The arts were lavishly funded and encouraged by Charlemagne, using imported artists where necessary, and Carolingian developments were decisive for the future course of [Western art](/wiki/Western_art "Western art"). [Carolingian illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/List_of_key_works_of_Carolingian_illumination "List of key works of Carolingian illumination") and ivory plaques, which have survived in reasonable numbers, approached those of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in quality. The main surviving monument of [Carolingian architecture](/wiki/Carolingian_architecture "Carolingian architecture") is the [Palatine Chapel in Aachen](/wiki/Palatine_Chapel_in_Aachen "Palatine Chapel in Aachen"), which is an impressive and confident adaptation of [San Vitale, Ravenna](/wiki/San_Vitale,_Ravenna "San Vitale, Ravenna") – from where some of the pillars were brought. Many other important buildings existed, such as the monasteries of Centula or [St Gall](/wiki/St_Gall "St Gall"), or the old [Cologne Cathedral](/wiki/Cologne_Cathedral "Cologne Cathedral"), since rebuilt. These large structures and complexes made frequent use of towers.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

A sizeable portion of the Frankish aristocracy quickly followed Clovis in converting to Christianity (the Frankish church of the Merovingians). The conversion of all under Frankish rule required a considerable amount of time and effort.

### Paganism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Paganism")]

Main article: [Frankish mythology](/wiki/Frankish_mythology "Frankish mythology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Childeric%27s_bees.jpg/250px-Childeric%27s_bees.jpg)](/wiki/File:Childeric%27s_bees.jpg)

Drawing of golden bees or flies that was discovered in the tomb of [Childeric I](/wiki/Childeric_I "Childeric I")

Echoes of [Frankish paganism](/wiki/Frankish_mythology "Frankish mythology") can be found in the primary sources, but their meaning is not always clear. Interpretations by modern scholars differ greatly, but it is likely that Frankish paganism shared most of the characteristics of other varieties of [Germanic paganism](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism"). The mythology of the Franks was probably a form of [Germanic polytheism](/wiki/Germanic_polytheism "Germanic polytheism"). It was highly ritualistic. Many daily activities centred around the multiple deities, chiefest of which may have been the [Quinotaur](/wiki/Quinotaur "Quinotaur"), a water-god from whom the Merovingians were reputed to have derived their ancestry.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Most of their gods were linked with local cult centres and their sacred character and power were associated with specific regions, outside of which they were neither worshipped nor feared. Most of the gods were "worldly", possessing form and having connections with specific objects, in contrast to the God of Christianity.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Frankish paganism has been observed in the burial site of Childeric I, where the king's body was found covered in a cloth decorated with numerous bees. There is a likely connection with the bees to the traditional Frankish weapon, the [angon](/wiki/Angon "Angon") (meaning "sting"), from its distinctive spearhead. It is possible that the [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") is derived from the angon.

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Christianity")]

Further information: [Christianity in Merovingian Gaul](/wiki/Christianity_in_Merovingian_Gaul "Christianity in Merovingian Gaul") and [Frankish Church](/wiki/Frankish_Church "Frankish Church")

Some Franks, like the 4th century usurper [Silvanus](/wiki/Claudius_Silvanus "Claudius Silvanus"), converted early to Christianity. In 496, Clovis I, who had married a Burgundian [Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") named [Clotilda](/wiki/Clotilde "Clotilde") in 493, was baptised by [Saint Remi](/wiki/Saint_Remi "Saint Remi") after a decisive victory over the Alemanni at the [Battle of Tolbiac](/wiki/Battle_of_Tolbiac "Battle of Tolbiac"). According to Gregory of Tours, over three thousand of his soldiers were baptised with him.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Clovis' conversion had a profound effect on the course of European history, for at the time the Franks were the only major [Christianised Germanic tribe](/wiki/Germanic_Christianity "Germanic Christianity") without a predominantly [Arian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") aristocracy and this led to a naturally amicable relationship between the Catholic Church and the increasingly powerful Franks.

Although many of the Frankish aristocracy quickly followed Clovis in converting to Christianity, the conversion of all his subjects was only achieved after considerable effort and, in some regions, a period of over two centuries.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The *Chronicle of St. Denis* relates that, following Clovis' conversion, a number of pagans who were unhappy with this turn of events rallied around [Ragnachar](/wiki/Ragnachar "Ragnachar"), who had played an important role in Clovis' initial rise to power. Although the text remains unclear as to the precise pretext, Clovis had Ragnachar executed.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Remaining pockets of resistance were overcome region by region, primarily due to the work of an expanding network of monasteries.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg/250px-Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg)

[Gelasian Sacramentary](/wiki/Gelasian_Sacramentary "Gelasian Sacramentary"), c. 750

The Merovingian Church was shaped by both internal and external forces. It had to come to terms with an established Gallo-Roman hierarchy that resisted changes to its culture, Christianise pagan sensibilities and suppress their expression, provide a new theological basis for Merovingian forms of kingship deeply rooted in pagan Germanic tradition and accommodate Irish and [Anglo-Saxon missionary](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_mission "Anglo-Saxon mission") activities and papal requirements.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The Carolingian reformation of monasticism and church-state relations was the culmination of the Frankish Church.

The increasingly wealthy Merovingian elite endowed many monasteries, including that of the Irish missionary [Columbanus](/wiki/Columbanus "Columbanus"). The 5th, 6th and 7th centuries saw two major waves of [hermitism](/wiki/Hermit "Hermit") in the Frankish world, which led to legislation requiring that all monks and hermits follow the [Rule of St Benedict](/wiki/Rule_of_St_Benedict "Rule of St Benedict").[[92]](#cite_note-92) The Church sometimes had an uneasy relationship with the Merovingian kings, whose claim to rule depended on a mystique of royal descent and who tended to revert to the polygamy of their pagan ancestors. Rome encouraged the Franks to slowly replace the [Gallican Rite](/wiki/Gallican_Rite "Gallican Rite") with the [Roman rite](/wiki/Roman_rite "Roman rite").

## Laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franks&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Laws")]

As with other Germanic peoples, the laws of the Franks were memorised by "rachimburgs", who were analogous to the [lawspeakers](/wiki/Lawspeaker "Lawspeaker") of [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

By the 6th century, when these laws first appeared in written form, two basic legal subdivisions existed: Salian Franks were subject to [Salic law](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law") and Ripuarian Franks to [Ripuarian law](/wiki/Lex_Ripuaria "Lex Ripuaria"). The Salic legal code applied in the Neustrian area from the river Liger ([Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire")) to the *[Silva Carbonaria](/wiki/Silva_Carbonaria "Silva Carbonaria")*, a forest south of present-day Brussels. It represented the boundary of the original area of Frankish settlement, which Chlodio pushed past in the 5th century..

The Ripuarian law was apparently used on the other side of the Silva Carbonaria, in the older Frankish kingdoms. The Rhineland or "Ripuarian" Franks who lived near the stretch of the Rhine from roughly [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") to [Duisburg](/wiki/Duisburg "Duisburg"), the region of the city of [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), are often considered separately from the Salians, and sometimes in modern texts referred to as Ripuarian Franks. The [Ravenna Cosmography](/wiki/Ravenna_Cosmography "Ravenna Cosmography") suggests that *Francia Renensis* included the old *civitas* of the [Ubii](/wiki/Ubii "Ubii"), in Germania II ([Germania Inferior](/wiki/Germania_Inferior "Germania Inferior")), but also the northern part of Germania I (Germania Superior), including [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"). Like the Salians they appear in Roman records both as raiders and as contributors to military units. Unlike the Salii, there is no record of when, if ever, the empire officially accepted their residence within its borders. They eventually succeeded to hold the city of Cologne, and at some point seem to have acquired the name Ripuarians, which may have meant "river people". In any case a Merovingian legal code was called the *[Lex Ribuaria](/wiki/Lex_Ribuaria "Lex Ribuaria")*, but it probably applied in all the older Frankish lands, including the original Salian areas.

Gallo-Romans south of the [River Loire](/wiki/Loire_River "Loire River") and the clergy remained subject to traditional [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law").[[94]](#cite_note-94) Germanic law was overwhelmingly concerned with the protection of individuals and less concerned with protecting the interests of the state. According to Michel Rouche, "Frankish judges devoted as much care to a case involving the theft of a dog as Roman judges did to cases involving the fiscal responsibility of *curiales*, or municipal councilors".[[95]](#cite_note-95)
